chunk_summary: Tele-nutrition supports moderate acute malnutrition rehabilitation for clinically stable children aged 6–59 months without danger signs, focusing on local foods, structured follow-ups, and escalation protocols for non-response.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for moderate acute malnutrition in children aged 6–59 months.
Learn tele-nutrition strategies for meal planning and energy-dense food integration.
Identify follow-up protocols and escalation criteria for non-responsive cases.
Explore the role of community services in supplementary nutrition linkage.
key_facts: Moderate acute malnutrition is defined as weight-for-height between -3 and -2 z-scores and/or MUAC ≥115 mm and <125 mm for children aged 6–59 months.
Tele-rehabilitation is appropriate for children who are active, feeding, and without oedema or systemic danger signs.
Follow-up includes weekly weight and MUAC checks, with a 2-week checkpoint for progress assessment.
Non-response is classified if recovery is not achieved within four months despite interventions.
Structured meal plans should include culturally acceptable, energy-dense foods like oil, ghee, nut paste, milk, curd, eggs, and legumes.
reference_quote: Baseline triage determines whether tele-only rehabilitation is appropriate. Tele-only support is reasonable when the child is active, feeding, without oedema, and without systemic danger signs. (Source: WHO, 9789240082830)
document_key_topics: Structured meal planning for malnourished children
Follow-up and escalation protocols for non-response
